entries.summary: GPLv3 introduced the GPL family's first explicit patent grant (§11) as a direct response to the 2006 Microsoft-Novell pact, which extended Microsoft's patent protection only to Novell's SUSE customers. Richard Stallman wrote that GPLv3 was designed to 'stop patent deals like Microsoft-Novell' and to provide 'explicit patent protection from the program's contributors and redistributors to the users.' The grant is broad but coupled with retaliation: initiating patent litigation against the Program triggers license termination, and distributing with a discriminatory patent license triggers conveyance restrictions.

sources.source: FSF — Richard Stallman, 'Why Upgrade to GPLv3'
sources.excerpt: Another threat that GPLv3 resists is that of patent deals like the Microsoft-Novell pact. … GPLv3 also provides users with explicit patent protection from the program's contributors and redistributors. With GPLv2, users rely on an implicit patent license to make sure that the company which provided them a copy won't sue them, or the people they redistribute copies to, for patent infringement.

entries.summary: GPLv3 §6 (Installation Information for User Products) explicitly prohibits the 'tivoization' pattern — shipping GPL-licensed software locked down to refuse modified versions via hardware-enforced signing. The FSF cited the TiVo Digital Video Recorder as the motivating example: the device ran modified Linux but prevented users from installing their own builds, which the FSF argued defeated the spirit of the license. The ban applies to consumer products but explicitly exempts embedded-industrial devices.

sources.source: FSF — Richard Stallman, 'Why Upgrade to GPLv3'
sources.excerpt: One major danger that GPLv3 will block is tivoization. … The motive for tivoization is that the software has features the distributor thinks some users will unfriendly — the distributor of your copy was to add them. … Tivoization is the way they deny you this; to make it a reality, they need to use hardware to stop you from running modified versions. The ban on tivoization applies to any product whose use by consumers is to be expected, even occasionally. GPLv3 tolerates tivoization only for products whose primary use is industrial or environmental.

entries.summary: GPLv3 is one-way compatible with Apache License 2.0: Apache-2.0 code can be incorporated into GPLv3 works, but the reverse is not allowed. The Apache Software Foundation states this compatibility arose because GPLv3 accepts the Apache-2.0 patent-termination and indemnification clauses that made Apache-2.0 incompatible with GPLv2. The ASF itself avoids GPLv3 code in Apache projects because linking would create a derivative work requiring GPLv3 distribution, which conflicts with the ASF's requirement that Apache projects ship under Apache-2.0.

sources.source: Apache Software Foundation — Apache License v2.0 and GPL Compatibility
sources.excerpt: Apache 2 software can therefore be included in GPLv3 projects, because the GPLv3 license accepts our software into GPLv3 works. However, GPLv3 software cannot be included in Apache projects. … We avoid GPLv3 software because merely linking to it is considered by the GPLv3 authors to create a derivative work. We want to honor their license. Unless GPLv3 licensors relax this interpretation of their own license regarding linking, Apache projects must not use GPLv3 software — that is, it is incompatible. This is an identical issue for both GPLv2 and GPLv3.

sources.summary: FSF's authoritative license-compatibility registry, which classifies GPLv3 as compatible with Apache-2.0 and enumerates other GPL-compatible licenses. Used as a cross-reference to the ASF statement.

entries.summary: GPLv3 is deliberately silent on network interaction — a design choice, not an oversight. Drafts of GPLv3 contemplated including an Affero-style clause requiring source disclosure to network users, but the FSF split this into the separate Affero GPL 3.0 because commercial free-software users raised administrative-cost concerns about having the requirement automatically apply to all GPL code. The GPL FAQ clarifies that mere network interaction is not 'conveying' under §0. Projects that want the network-use-disclosure trigger must license under AGPLv3 explicitly.

sources.source: FSF — GPL FAQ, 'Why did you decide to write the GNU Affero GPLv3 as a separate license?'
sources.excerpt: Early drafts of GPLv3 allowed licensors to add an Affero-like requirement to publish source in section 7. However, some companies that develop and rely upon free software consider this requirement to be too burdensome. They want to avoid code with this requirement, and expressed concern about the administrative costs of checking code for this additional requirement. By publishing the GNU Affero GPLv3 as a separate license, with provisions in it and GPLv3 to allow code under these licenses to link to each other, we accomplish all of our original goals while making it easier to determine which code has the source publication requirement.

sources.source: FSF — Richard Stallman, 'Why Upgrade to GPLv3'
Apache Software Foundation — Apache License v2.0 and GPL Compatibility
